flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight is removed; the disclosed jurisdictional-preservation proviso is itself a power-narrowing feature. (Palmyra was previously part of the EO 8198 Zombie Emergency Trap family of Pacific-island reservations; this order is a distinct interagency jurisdiction transfer, not an emergency-trap mechanism itself.)

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Purely definitional commodity-designation order (bromine, cobalt, abrasives, precision-measuring machinery, aviation-lubricating-oil production equipment, etc.) for an already-issued Presidential proclamation, applying already-existing export-control Regulations 2-12 (issued July 2, 1940) by reference — matching the treatment of EO 8607.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No new discretion or check-removal; applies a pre-existing regulatory framework by reference, matching EO 8607.

title: Executive Order 8618—Ordering Certain Units and Members of the National Guard of the United States Into the Active Military Service of the United States

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Fifth and largest National Guard federalization order in this stream to date (matching EO 8530, EO 8551, EO 8594, EO 8605), federalizing numerous coast-artillery, field-artillery, cavalry, tank, and two full infantry divisions (26th, 38th) across four staggered January 1941 effective dates, pursuant to the same explicit congressional authorization.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Clear statutory basis, itemized units and members, disclosed staggered effective dates and service terms.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Bundles many named units into one order, but a single coherent mobilization action, matching prior treatment.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Explicit twelve-consecutive-month term ('unless sooner relieved') — a genuine, disclosed bounding provision, matching prior instances.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight is removed; implements Congress's own contemporaneous authorization.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Fifth and largest instance in this stream's mass National Guard federalization pattern, spanning August 1940-January 1941 and now encompassing two full divisions in a single order — the cumulative scale of this pattern by year-end 1940 is itself a significant pre-Pearl-Harbor mobilization marker.

title: Executive Order 8619—Designating the Director of Selective Service To Perform Certain Functions and Duties Under the Selective Training and Service Act of 1940

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Delegates to the Director of Selective Service 'all of the functions and duties vested in the President...that relate to appeals from the determinations of boards of appeal' — this removes the one distinctly presidential-level check identified in this stream's analysis of EO 8560 (the narrow dependency-hardship appeal-to-the-President mechanism), merging final appellate authority over draft classifications with the same official who administers the system day-to-day.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The delegation is itself disclosed and public, with the underlying Selective Service Regulations 'modified accordingly' on the order's face — a transparent, not concealed, structural change.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Diff-aware, structurally significant finding: directly eliminates the specific, previously-identified presidential-level review layer disclosed in EO 8560 ('An appeal from a determination of a board of appeal may be made to the President, only if...') by wholesale delegation to the Director — a citable, concrete removal of a previously-established check, not merely a hypothetical gap.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A quiet but structurally significant regression in the appellate integrity of the CRITICAL-rated draft-classification system (EO 8560): the single point of genuinely independent, non-administrative review for the most sympathetic hardship cases is folded into the same administrative hierarchy it was designed to check, illustrating this stream's 'quiet regression' pattern in one of its highest-stakes systems.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture

title: Executive Order 8623—Suspension of Eight-Hour Law as to Persons Employed by the Government in the Construction of Certain Army and Navy Bases in British Possessions in the Atlantic Ocean

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Exercises a narrow, specific statutory exception already built into the underlying 1892/1913 Eight-Hour Law itself ('except in case of extraordinary emergency'), rather than creating independent new authority; the order includes an explicit, disclosed factual finding of extraordinary emergency (labor-market isolation at the leased bases, local labor-practice conflicts).

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Extensive, disclosed factual findings and reasoning ('Whereas' clauses) justify the emergency determination — a transparent, reasoned order.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Suspends a federal worker-hour protection for laborers at isolated overseas construction sites with limited local labor-market alternatives — a genuine, if narrowly justified, structural opening for overwork given the workers' limited bargaining position in this isolated context.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: No stated termination date or review trigger for the suspension; tied to the open-ended construction/national-defense need at these bases.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Exercises a specific, narrow statutory exception already anticipated and provided by Congress itself, not a removal of an oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Historically significant: these are the actual British-possession bases acquired under the September 1940 Destroyers-for-Bases Agreement — an early, concrete instance of American overseas military construction and wartime labor-standard suspension predating Pearl Harbor by a year.
